title: Discontinuous BBP transitions
authors: Dario Bocchi, Giulio Biroli, Chiara Cammarota, Federico Ricci-Tersenghi
abstract: The Baik-Ben Arous-Peche (BBP) transition sets fundamental limits for detecting low-rank structure in noisy high-dimensional data and underlies a wide range of spectral methods in many fields from physics to statistics and data sciences. In standard settings, this transition is continuous, implying that signal recovery emerges gradually above a sharp threshold. We show that BBP transitions can instead be discontinuous in very general settings and provide a full theory of this phenomenon. When the eigenvalue density vanishes faster than linearly at the spectral edge, the overlap between the leading eigenvector and the signal jumps discontinuously at the critical point. We study this mechanism in deformed Gaussian and reweighted Wishart ensembles. We analyze in detail the finite-size effects, which play a central and qualitatively new role in the discontinuous BBP transition. Unlike the continuous BBP transition, we establish the existence of an extended pre-critical region where informative eigenvectors emerge well before the asymptotic threshold. The main consequence-and difference from the continuous BBP transition-is that signal recovery can occur at significantly lower signal-to-noise ratio and it is accompanied by strong sample-to-sample variability. Our results show the relevance and the novelty of the discontinuous BBP transition, and highlight the practical implications for signal detection.
summary: This paper identifies a new class of discontinuous BBP transitions in random matrices where signal detection occurs via an abrupt jump in overlap. It reveals that finite-size effects can actually lower the detection threshold, allowing signals to be recovered even below the theoretical asymptotic limit.
main_problem: The paper investigates the conditions under which the Baik-Ben Arous-Péché (BBP) transition, which governs the detection of low-rank structures in noisy data, becomes discontinuous rather than continuous.
main_result: The authors establish that a discontinuous BBP transition occurs when the eigenvalue density vanishes faster than linearly at the spectral edge (exponent a > 2), leading to an abrupt jump in eigenvector overlap and a finite-size 'pre-critical' region for signal recovery.
method: The study employs the resolvent formalism, Stieltjes transform analysis, and free probability theory to analyze the spectral properties of large random matrices.
model_or_system: The research focuses on spiked random matrix models, specifically deformed Gaussian Orthogonal Ensembles (GOE) with diagonal disorder and reweighted Wishart ensembles.
key_observables: Eigenvalue density, eigenvector overlap (correlation between the leading eigenvector and the signal), and the outlier eigenvalue position.
important_parameters: Spike strength (alpha), spectral edge power-law exponent (a), matrix size (N), and the ratio of dimensions (q).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the continuous spectrum occupies a single interval and focuses on the behavior at the spectral edge in the large-N limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares continuous vs. discontinuous BBP transitions via the inverse resolvent and overlap; Figure 2 shows the z(x) curve for deformed GOE; Figure 4 demonstrates finite-size scaling of the maximum eigenvalue.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the BBP transition, defines the condition for discontinuity via spectral density decay, analyzes specific ensembles (deformed GOE and Wishart), develops a finite-size scaling theory, and discusses implications for machine learning and biological systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Continuous vs. Discontinuous BBP transitions. Each row shows (left to right) the inverse resolvent map z(g), the spectral density near the right edge, and the eigenvector overlap as a function of the spike strength. In the continuous case, z(g) develops a turning point (dz/dg = 0) at the edge, beyond which lies the incorrect branch, the density vanishes sub-linearly, and the overlap turns on smoothly. In the discontinuous case, z(g) reaches the edge without a turning point (dz/dg ̸= 0), the density vanishes super-linearly at the edge, and the overlap exhibits a finite jump at onset.

FIG. 2: The origin of different tails of spectral densities in the Deformed GOE ensemble. The function z(x) is plotted against the auxiliary variable x = g −z/σ2 for different values of a and hmax. In the first line, the plot for a standard GOE (hmax = 0) is shown. Left column (a < 2): The function z(x) always exhibits a turning point (z′(x) = 0) and the incorrect branch diverges at the domain boundary xc = −hmax/σ2, implying a standard square-root edge in all three cases. Right column (a > 2): The derivative z′(x) is finite at the domain boundary, and depending on the values of hmax and σ can be positive (mid panel), leading again to a square-root edge, or negative (bottom panel), leading...

FIG. 3: The origin of different tails of spectral densities in the Reweighted Wishart ensemble. The function z(g) is plotted for different values of a and the aspect ratio q = N/T. Left column (a < 2): The branch with incorrect positive derivative z′(g), diverging at the domain boundary gmax = (qfmax)−1, ensures the existence of a turning point with z′(g) = 0 and consequently a standard square-root edge. Right column (a > 2): The derivative z′(g) is finite at the domain boundary, and depending on the value of q can be positive (top panel), allowing for a turning point and a square-root edge, or negative (bottom panel), leading to a super-linear edge.

FIG. 4: Finite-size scaling of the maximum eigenvalue. Upper panels are for the deformed GOE with parameters a = 3, hmax = 5, and σ2 = 2. Lower panels are for the reweighted Wishart ensemble with parameter a = 3, fmax = 1, and q = 5. In the left panels, we plot the difference between the mean value of largest eigenvalue λmax (averaged over 104 samples) and the theoretical edge λ+ computed in the large N limit, as a function of the distance from the critical point, α −αBBP. In the right panels, the same data is collapsed using the predicted scaling for both axes. The data collapse confirms that the fluctuations of the largest eigenvalue and the width of the critical region scale as N −1/a,...

FIG. 5: Finite-size scaling of the overlap. Upper panels are for the deformed GOE with parameters a = 3, hmax = 5, and σ2 = 2. Lower panels are for the reweighted Wishart ensemble with parameter a = 3, fmax = 1, and q = 5. In the left panels, we plot the mean overlap between the principal eigenvector and the rank-one perturbation (i.e., the signal to be detected), as a function of the distance from the critical point, α −αBBP. In the right panels, the same data is collapsed using the predicted scaling.
